content.full_text:
LAURENCE J. SCHMIT
SUPERVISOR, SECOND DISTRICT

ORANGE COUNTY ADMINISTRATION BUILDING
515 NORTH SYCAMORE, P.O. BOX 667, SANTA ANA, CALIFORNIA 92702

PHONE: 834-3220 (AREA CODE 714)

February 7, 1977

Ted Prim, Staff Counsel
State of California Fair Political
Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Prim:

Thank you for your letter of February 1, 1977, discribing
your conversations of the last two weeks with my Executive
Assistant, Steve Knoblock.

I appreciate your letter and have forwarded it to the
treasurer of the committee, Mr. Ray Edwards, Certified
Public Accountant, who files all of my campaign statements.

For your information, any communications should be properly
addressed to Mr. Edwards at 2021 East Fourth Street, Santa
Ana, California. If you need to contact Mr. Edwards by
phone, he can be reached at (714) 542-4745.

Thank you again for your letter.

Sincerely,

LAURENCE J. SCHMIT
Supervisor, Second District

kvw

State of California

Memorandum

To     :     File                                                           Date  :  Feb. 7, 1977




From  :     FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
            Ted Prim

Subject:    Letter to Steve Knoblock

            His question, and therefore my advice, related only to
            an 82013(a) committee which was the controlled committee
            of a candidate.

            TP:jo

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P O BOX 617 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

February 1, 1977


Mr. Steve Knoblock
515 North Sycamore
Santa Ana, CA 92702

Dear Mr. Knoblock:

     This letter is to confirm our conversations of the
last two weeks and to clarify your reporting alternatives.
As I indicated, currently there is no procedure under the
Political Reform Act or pursuant to Commission regulations
by which a committee may formally terminate its existence.
However, the Commission staff has informally advised filers
in the past that they may effectively terminate by means of
a two-step process. First, the committee must eliminate all
debts or obligations, and dispense with all funds. Second,
the treasurer should send to the Secretary of State and each
person who receives a copy of the committee's final campaign
statement a letter which states that the committee has elimi-
nated all debts or obligations, dispensed with all funds, and
ceased to receive contributions or make expenditures and does
not anticipate doing so in the future. The letter also should
advise the recipient that future campaign statements will not
be filed.

     If all of these steps are not carried out the com-
mittee will remain a committee even though it may not possess
any present reporting obligations. For example, if the com-
mittee in question receives no contributions and makes no ex-
penditures after the closing date for its January, 1977, semi-
annual statement, it would not have to file further semiannual
statements but it, nevertheless, would be required to file
three campaign statements in connection with the candidate's
next election.

     From my conversations with the Orange County Regis-
trar, it appears that stops sufficient to terminate the

[3]

Mr. Steve Knobruck
Page Two
February 1, 1977




existence of the committee have not been taken.  It is my under-
standing that the loans in question are listed as unpaid loans.
However, if the committee is to terminate, the loan debts must
be forgiven and the forgiveness must be listed as contributions.
Thus, if the statement is not amended the committee will remain
a committee with future filing obligations.

     If you have any further questions, please contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Ted Prim
                                        Ted Prim
                                        Staff Counsel
                                        Legal Division

TP:jo

cc:  Ms. Jeanette Shelton
     Office of the Registrar of Voters
     P.O. Box 11298
     Santa Ana, CA  92711

     Natalie

sections.question: What procedure allows a committee to formally terminate its existence under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion: There is no formal procedure under the Political Reform Act for committee termination, but the Commission staff has informally advised that committees may effectively terminate through a two-step process: (1) eliminate all debts/obligations and dispense with all funds (with loan forgiveness counted as contributions), and (2) send a letter to the Secretary of State and campaign statement recipients stating the committee has ceased operations and will not file future statements. If these steps are not completed, the committee remains active with future filing obligations.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks about the procedures for formally terminating a candidate's controlled committee under the Political Reform Act, specifically regarding the handling of debts and reporting obligations.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that while no formal termination procedure exists, committees can effectively terminate by eliminating all debts (treating forgiveness as contributions), dispensing with funds, and notifying relevant parties in writing. Any failure to complete these steps means the committee remains active for filing purposes.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple letters: (1) Supervisor Schmit's response letter referring matter to his campaign treasurer, (2) FPPC internal memo noting the advice related to section 82013(a) committees, and (3) the substantive FPPC advice letter from Ted Prim to Steve Knoblock dated February 1, 1977. The question and conclusion are synthesized from the substantive advice letter content. No formal 'QUESTION' and 'CONCLUSION' section headers present, but question and answer are clearly demarcated in the advice letter body.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What procedure allows a committee to formally terminate its existence under the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: There is no formal procedure under the Political Reform Act for committee termination, but the Commission staff has informally advised that committees may effectively terminate through a two-step process: (1) eliminate all debts/obligations and dispense with all funds (with loan forgiveness counted as contributions), and (2) send a letter to the Secretary of State and campaign statement recipients stating the committee has ceased operations and will not file future statements. If these steps are not completed, the committee remains active with future filing obligations.

embedding.summary: FPPC Staff Counsel Ted Prim provides advice to Steve Knoblock (via Steve Knoblock and Supervisor Laurence J. Schmit) regarding the process for terminating a candidate's controlled committee under California's Political Reform Act, outlining the two-step informal procedure required and the consequences of non-compliance.
